insights: No statistically significant differences exist in resolution times across the five agents (Kruskal-Wallis p=0.2945), indicating that observed numerical variations (124–158 days mean) reflect random sampling variation rather than true performance gaps.

Only 47.2% of records (236 out of 500) have valid positive resolution times, with 52.8% exhibiting closed_at timestamps preceding opened_at — a systemic data integrity issue affecting all agents uniformly (invalid rates: 46%–58%).

Critical and High priority tickets show the highest invalid resolution time rate (57.6%), suggesting timestamp capture failures are more prevalent in urgent cases — potentially undermining SLA tracking and escalation reliability.

The largest observed agent-level gap (Fred Luddy at 2984 hours vs. Charlie Whitherspoon at 3801 hours) represents a 34-day difference but is not statistically distinguishable from noise, confirming that individual agent targeting would misallocate optimization effort.

All agents handle comparable case complexity distributions, and the absence of statistical significance across priority, category, and assignment group strata reinforces that resolution time variability is driven by systemic process or data capture issues — not agent capability or workload composition.

Network category exhibits the highest invalid resolution time rate (57%), while Software shows the lowest (48%), indicating category-specific data entry or integration inconsistencies that may distort root cause analysis for high-frequency incident types.
summary: The analysis definitively refutes the premise of meaningful agent-level performance discrepancies: resolution time differences across the five agents are not statistically significant (p=0.2945), and the observed 34-day gap between best- and worst-performing agents falls within expected sampling variation. Instead, the dominant constraint is data integrity — over half of all records (52.8%) contain chronologically invalid timestamps, with higher-priority and Network-related tickets disproportionately affected. This systemic issue undermines the reliability of any agent-, category-, or SLA-based performance assessment. Optimization efforts must therefore shift from individual agent coaching to addressing upstream data capture processes — particularly for high-priority tickets and Network incidents — and rebuilding trust in timestamp accuracy before meaningful resolution time benchmarking or trend analysis can proceed.
